title: Doomscrolling Rage and Information Hangover
dek: *When you scroll through bad news for an hour and wake up exhausted, you're not lazy or weak-willed. You're carrying unfelt anger in your nervous system, and your body is trying to metabolize what your mind refuses to acknowledge. Here's how to interrupt the cycle and find your way back to presence.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Doomscrolling floods your nervous system with cortisol while your conscious mind pretends you're just "staying informed." The anger you don't feel during those scroll sessions migrates into your body as tension, insomnia, and a cognitive fog we call information hangover. Learning to name the rage underneath the compulsion is the first step toward reclaiming your attention and your rest.

She sat on the couch at 11:47 p.m., thumb moving in small circles across the glass, each swipe revealing another headline about something burning or breaking or being taken away. Her jaw was set, her shoulders high and tight, her breath shallow in the top of her chest. She told herself she needed to know, needed to stay aware, needed to understand what was happening in the world. But her body was telling a different story. Her heart rate had climbed fifteen beats per minute. Her stomach had gone sour. And somewhere beneath the numb glaze of her eyes, a wordless fury was building that she would not name, would not feel, would not let into the room. She scrolled for another forty minutes, then wondered why she couldn't sleep. ## The Scroll That Doesn't Satisfy Doomscrolling is not really about information. It's a repetitive behavior that mimics purposeful research but delivers something else entirely: a steady drip of cortisol, adrenaline, and unmetabolized emotional charge. You open the app to "check in." Thirty minutes later you're three years deep into a comment thread about a crisis you can't solve, and your hands are cold. The term entered common usage during the pandemic, but the pattern is older. It's the same neural pathway that kept our ancestors scanning the horizon for threats, except now the horizon is infinite and the threats are algorithmically chosen to keep you engaged. Your brain doesn't distinguish between a real predator and a enraging news story. Both trigger the same ancient wiring. Robert Augustus Masters writes about the difference between anger that moves through the body and anger that gets trapped in repetitive mental loops. When you scroll through content that provokes you but offers no avenue for response, your anger has nowhere to go. It doesn't discharge. It doesn't resolve. It circles back into your system as tension, hypervigilance, and a low-grade sense of threat that follows you into the next day. In my own practice, I've noticed that clients who doomscroll before bed almost always report fragmented sleep, vivid or distressing dreams, and a feeling of waking up "already tired." That's not coincidence. That's your nervous system trying to process a backlog of rage it wasn't allowed to feel in real time. ## Information Hangover: The Cognitive Afterburn An information hangover is what happens when you consume more emotional stimulus than your nervous system can digest. You wake up foggy. Your focus is scattered. You feel irritable but can't name why. You reach for coffee, but the fatigue isn't really physical. It's the residue of anger you scrolled past without acknowledging. The term "hangover" is apt because the mechanism is similar. Alcohol floods your system with a substance your liver has to work overtime to clear. Doomscrolling floods your system with cortisol, and your adrenal glands have to work overtime to regulate. Both leave you depleted, dehydrated at a cellular level, and craving relief you can't quite find. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to the idea that unfelt emotion doesn't vanish. It migrates. It shows up as insomnia, as digestive trouble, as that clenched feeling in your chest that you've learned to ignore. Information hangover is what it feels like when your body is trying to tell you that your anger needs attention, and you keep refreshing the feed instead. Thich Nhat Hanh taught that anger is like a crying baby. It needs to be held, not hidden. When you scroll through content that enrages you and then immediately swipe to the next thing, you're refusing to hold the baby. You're piling more babies on top of each other until the whole stack is wailing and you can't hear anything clearly. ## The Rage You're Not Supposed to Feel There's a quiet cultural script that says staying informed is virtuous and anger is dangerous. So you scroll through injustice after injustice, telling yourself you're being a responsible citizen, and you push the fury down into your gut. You're not allowed to throw your phone. You're not allowed to scream. You're not even allowed to admit that you're furious, because fury feels too big, too loud, too much. Harriet Lerner points out that women especially are socialized to suppress anger, to redirect it into worry or sadness or self-blame. Doomscrolling is a perfect container for that suppression. You get to feel like you're doing something (scrolling, reading, "staying aware") while never actually letting yourself feel the full weight of your rage. But your body knows. Your shoulders know. Your jaw knows. And the PubMed research on anger expression and cardiovascular load makes it clear: suppressed anger doesn't protect your heart. It burdens it. The scroll becomes a kind of emotional buffer zone. You're close enough to the anger to feel its heat, but not close enough to let it burn through you and transform into something else. You're stuck in the zone of stimulation without resolution, and that's where the hangover lives. ## The Body Keeps the Scroll Pay attention to what happens in your body when you doomscroll. Not what you think about it. What you *feel*. Your breath gets shallow, high in the chest, sometimes held entirely for seconds at a time. Your shoulders creep toward your ears. Your hands get cold because blood is shunting toward your core, preparing you for a fight that never comes. Your pupils dilate slightly. Your digestion slows. Your pelvic floor clenches. All of this is your body preparing to *do* something with the anger, and then you just... keep scrolling. The disconnect between arousal and action is where the poison accumulates. Your system is revved, but there's no release. No running, no shouting, no physical discharge. Just more input. More headlines. More comment threads. More reasons to be furious that you're not allowed to fully feel. Over the years I've seen this show up as chronic neck pain, as TMJ, as that strange全身 exhaustion that no amount of sleep seems to touch. The body keeps the scroll. It remembers every swipe you made while holding your breath, every story you read while clenching your teeth, every moment of rage you swallowed instead of naming. ## Breaking the Loop: A Five-Minute Practice Here's something you can do right now, especially if you've just realized you've been scrolling for longer than you meant to. Put the phone down. Physically place it across the room, screen-down. Stand up. Shake out your hands like you're flinging water off them. Do this for thirty seconds. Vigorous, loose, silly if you need to be. Let your jaw hang open. Let your shoulders drop. Now place both hands on your belly and take three breaths deep enough that your hands move. Out loud, say: "I'm angry." You don't have to know why. You don't have to justify it. Just name it. "I'm angry about what I just read. I'm angry about what's happening. I'm angry and I'm allowed to be." Then ask yourself: what does this anger want? Not what you should do about the news. What does the anger in *your body* need right now? Sometimes it's to move. Sometimes it's to cry. Sometimes it's to write three pages of furious illegible scrawl that no one will ever see. Sometimes it's to step outside and feel cold air on your face. Do that thing. For five minutes, let the anger move. Then check in with your body again. Notice if your breath is deeper. Notice if your hands are warmer. Notice if the compulsion to pick the phone back up has softened, even a little. ## Herbal Allies for the Aftermath If you're trying to recover from an information hangover, your nervous system needs support, not more stimulation. Adaptogens and nervines can help your body recalibrate without numbing you out. [NOW Foods Inositol Powder (1 lb)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B007XA489O?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a gentle option for supporting neurotransmitter balance, especially if your sleep has been disrupted by late-night scrolling. It doesn't sedate you. It helps your brain remember what calm feels like. For those nights when your mind won't stop replaying the headlines, [Gaia Herbs SleepThru](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005H1ENTQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) combines passionflower and valerian in a form that supports staying asleep, not just falling asleep. It's for the 3 a.m. wake-up where your heart is racing and you don't know why until you remember what you read six hours earlier. [Yogi Relaxed Mind (96 ct)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00404XCLQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a daily-drinker tea that won't knock you out but will take the edge off the hypervigilance. Ashwagandha and gotu kola work slowly, cumulatively, to help your system stop treating every notification like an emergency. You can explore more options in [our herbal cabinet](/herbs), where we break down which plants support which aspects of nervous system recovery. But remember: herbs are support, not replacement. They work best when you're also addressing the behavior that's depleting you in the first place. ## Rage as Information, Not Pathology What if your anger about the state of the world isn't something to manage away? What if it's accurate data about systems that are genuinely broken, and your body is responding appropriately to genuine threat? The problem isn't the anger. The problem is the scroll that keeps you in a state of stimulation without giving you any way to *use* the anger. Anger is energy. It's meant to move you toward boundary-setting, toward action, toward change. When you trap it in an endless scroll, it curdles into despair or numbness or that flat, hopeless feeling that makes you reach for the phone again. [The fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) on this site includes practices for channeling anger into creative and relational work, for letting it clarify your values instead of clouding your vision. Rage can be generative. It can be the fuel for art, for organizing, for the kind of clear "no" that protects what you love. But first you have to let yourself feel it, and that means stepping away from the feed. We also offer [assessments](/assessments) that can help you identify your specific anger patterns, how you've learned to suppress or redirect or discharge it. Knowing your pattern is half the work. The other half is practicing something different. ## When the World Is On Fire and You Can't Look Away There's a real ethical tension here. How do you stay informed without destroying your nervous system? How do you care about what's happening without drowning in it? The answer isn't to stop caring. It's to recognize that your capacity to respond to crisis is directly tied to the health of your nervous system. When you're depleted, foggy, running on fumes and suppressed rage, you can't think clearly. You can't organize. You can't show up for the people and causes that need you. Boundaries around information consumption aren't about sticking your head in the sand. They're about sustaining your capacity to be useful. You can set specific times to check the news. You can choose one or two trusted sources instead of scrolling fifteen. You can ask yourself, before you open the app: "Will this help me take meaningful action, or will it just rile me up with no outlet?" Sometimes the most radical thing you can do is close the phone, feel your anger fully, and then ask: what's mine to do? Not what's happening everywhere to everyone, but what's in front of you, in your sphere of influence, where your specific gifts and your specific fury can actually make a difference. For more on this kind of discernment, the companion practices at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) explore how to stay spiritually grounded while remaining engaged with the world's pain. It's not about bypassing. It's about resourcing. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### How do I know if I'm doomscrolling or just staying informed? If you finish your scroll session feeling more anxious, exhausted, or hopeless than when you started, and you can't name a single meaningful action you're going to take with the information you consumed, that's doomscrolling. Staying informed has a purpose and an endpoint. Doomscrolling is a loop. Your body will tell you the difference if you pause long enough to ask. ### Can I really be angry about things that aren't happening directly to me? Yes. Empathy and moral outrage are human. But there's a difference between anger that clarifies your values and propels you toward action, and anger that you consume passively until it poisons you. You're allowed to be furious about injustice. You're also allowed to protect your nervous system so that your fury can be useful instead of just corrosive. ### What if I feel guilty for not staying constantly updated? That guilt is often a signal that you've internalized the idea that your worth is tied to how much suffering you can witness. It's not. Your worth is inherent, and your capacity to create change in the world depends on you being resourced, not depleted. You can care deeply and still have boundaries. In fact, boundaries are what make sustained care possible. ## The Morning After the Scroll You will wake up some mornings and realize you did it again. You scrolled past your bedtime, you swallowed your rage, you woke up tired and foggy and already halfway to reaching for the phone. That's okay. This isn't about perfection. It's about noticing. Notice what it costs you. Notice the tightness in your chest, the ache in your jaw, the way your attention splinters before you've even had coffee. Notice, and then make one small choice differently. Put the phone in another room. Take three breaths before you open the app. Name the anger out loud, just once, just to yourself. The scroll will always be there. The outrage will always be waiting. But so will your body, your breath, your capacity to feel and move and choose. So will the people you love, the work that's yours to do, the specific corner of the world where your presence actually matters. The screen can wait. Your life is happening now, in this room, in this body, with this one wild and irreplaceable chance to be here fully. Let the anger teach you. Let it clarify what you're here to protect. And then step away from the feed and tend to what's alive. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on doomscrolling rage and information hangover, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
